description: src/lib/server/addressWatcher.ts: alreadyNotified()/recordTxid() key strictly on (wallet_kind, wallet_id, txid) — matching the notified_txids table's UNIQUE constraint (db.ts) — deliberately omitting user_id ('First sighting wins the insert'). Multisigs are single-owner today (docs/COLLABORATIVE-CUSTODY-PLAN.md §1: 'no sharing concept exists'), so this is latent, not yet exploitable. But the moment multisig_shares wiring lands (tracked in cairn-fvf5) and a wallet has multiple collaborators, a deposit to that shared wallet will insert exactly one notified_txids row (tied to the multisig's single user_id column, the only source addressWatcher.ts uses), and every OTHER collaborator will simply never receive a tx_received/tx_confirmed notification for that txid — silently, with no error or log signal. Fix: include user_id in the notified_txids UNIQUE constraint and in alreadyNotified()/recordTxid()'s key, so dedup is per-recipient, not per-wallet. Flag this to whoever wires up Unit 3/collaborative sharing so it's fixed before multi-user notification delivery is expected to work.
close_reason: Widened notified_txids dedup to be per-recipient: UNIQUE now (wallet_kind, wallet_id, user_id, txid); alreadyNotified() keys on user_id; baseline seen-set key includes user_id. Added a guarded table-rebuild migration for existing DBs (lossless under single-owner). Prevents silent tx-notification suppression for co-owners once collaborative custody ships. Note: full per-user delivery for SHARED wallets also needs the byScripthash watcher model to emit one Watched per collaborator — that remains collaborative-custody (Unit 3) work.

title: Mempool visualizer + Esplora cache minor perf cleanup
description:
Two small, unrelated-but-minor items bundled together:
1. src/lib/mempoolViz.ts synthesizeBlocks() recomputes the full squarified-treemap layout from scratch on every 10s poll tick in explorer/mempool/blocks/+page.svelte, even when underlying histogram/projected data has not meaningfully changed. Memoize by a hash of the inputs to skip recompute and avoid unnecessary DOM/transition churn.
2. src/lib/server/chain/esplora.ts in-memory TTL cache keys on the raw request path string with no normalization -- fine today since paths are built consistently, but worth a URL-object-based normalization pass to make it robust against future callers.
close_reason: Fixed in a0eb553: synthKey() fingerprint skips treemap re-synthesis and DOM churn when a poll returns an unchanged mempool picture; esplora cache keys normalized (duplicate slashes collapsed, trailing slash stripped) in the private get()

title: Signing mass UI: labels, warnings, consolidation suggestion
description: CoinControl: per-UTXO mass chip (low/medium/high) + 'Mining pool payout (high mass)' vs 'P2P transfer' source labels via utxo-mass endpoint (lazy fetch on disclosure open). Send flow (wallets page ONLY — vault send owned elsewhere): Review-step warning when mass is high ('includes UTXOs from large batch payouts... signing may take several minutes'), per-device time estimates, split suggestion ('consider 2 separate transactions'). Wallet detail: consolidation suggestion when high-mass UTXOs exist — 'send to yourself to make future signing easier', cost (consolidation fee estimate) vs benefit, one-click prefill of a self-send with those UTXOs selected in coin control.
close_reason: Committed: CoinControl mass chips + legend, Review signing-time panel (warnLevel-toned, exact spec copy incl. 'the network fee doesn't'), ConsolidationCard with set-hash dismissal and ?consolidate= handoff (preselect + Max + self-address prefill). Types are type-only re-exports of the server contract so shapes cannot drift. 581/581 tests; amber/red panels + consolidation flow live-verified in preview.

title: Jade air-gapped QR (BC-UR) signer — follow-on
description: Deferred from cairn-vxi/cairn-asn per docs/HARDWARE-PLAN.md §2.2 (Unit E2). Jade's QR mode uses BC-UR (Blockchain Commons Uniform Resources), a DIFFERENT codec from Cairn's existing BBQr — needs a separate hw/jadeUr.ts encoder/decoder + JadeQrSigner components. Scoped as its own release; Jade USB (Web Serial) shipped in cairn-vxi. Vet bc-ur vs @keystonehq/bc-ur-registry before building.
close_reason: Recovered + committed in d457e16: hw/jadeUr.ts BC-UR codec + JadeQrSigner.svelte + jade-qr device type wiring; 13 tests pass, svelte-check clean (no new errors).

title: No SPV/merkle verification of Electrum data; hostile server can forge payment notifications
description:
Security audit 2026-07-05 (docs/SECURITY-AUDIT-2026-07-05.md), finding F3. Verified CONFIRMED by 3/3 independent adversarial reviewers.

Cairn never verifies Electrum-server-reported transaction history/balance/UTXO data via any SPV/merkle-proof mechanism -- no call to blockchain.transaction.get_merkle exists anywhere in the codebase (src/lib/server/electrum/client.ts implements only get_balance/get_history/listunspent/broadcast/get_transaction/estimatefee/subscribe methods). src/lib/server/addressWatcher.ts handleScripthashChange() (~lines 286-376) reacts to an unauthenticated scripthash push from the Electrum socket and fires tx_received/tx_large push notifications (fanning out to email/telegram/ntfy/webhook/nostr via notify()) based purely on this unverified data, with no independent cross-check.

Nuance found during verification: the notification amount specifically is sourced from a second, separately-configured Esplora HTTP backend (chain/index.ts getTx() -> esplora.ts), not from Electrum's own transaction.get. A hostile Electrum server acting alone (Esplora untouched) can only produce a fake txid plus a generic amount-less 'New wallet activity' notice (the Esplora lookup legitimately 404s on a nonexistent txid). A FULLY convincing fake 'X BTC received' notification with an attacker-chosen amount requires the attacker to also control or spoof the configured Esplora endpoint -- realistic for a full network MITM (both defaults, electrum.blockstream.info + mempool.space, are reachable over the same network path) or for self-hosted deployments pairing one Electrum + one Esplora instance under a single compromised/malicious operator.

Impact: this is primarily a social-engineering/fraud-enablement risk (tricking a user or merchant into believing a payment cleared when it did not) rather than a direct fund-loss bug -- nothing in the reviewed code shows this feeding into balance display used for spending decisions or PSBT construction. Compounded by the companion finding that Electrum TLS certificate validation is disabled (cairn-azei), which widens the MITM surface needed to pull this off.

Fix: implement classic Electrum SPV verification (blockchain.transaction.get_merkle + header-chain validation) before trusting get_history/getTx results for any user-facing notification; the fix needs to cover both the Electrum history path and the Esplora amount/confirmation path identified above.
close_reason: Fixed in security-audit remediation pass; see commit. All 897 tests pass, typecheck clean.

title: Referrals: signing flow buy + troubleshooting links
description: Across TrezorSigner/LedgerSigner/BitboxSigner/JadeUsbSigner.svelte (src/lib/components/signing/) and the send-flow ColdCard/QR/Jade-QR signer components: show a 'buy a device' referral link when device is unavailable/not-detected, and a hardcoded official troubleshooting link (from src/lib/referrals.ts OFFICIAL_SUPPORT_URLS) in error states (e.g. Trezor form-error div ~line 265-275). Two distinct link types, both gated by referral_links flag except troubleshooting links which per scope are always-on (component 2 is not admin-configurable) -- confirm troubleshooting links render even if referral_links flag is off, since they're not really 'referral' content.
close_reason: Built + committed: schema/flag/tabs in 024542f, full feature (referrals constants + server helpers, /admin/referral-settings, wizard buy links, managed-service card, signer buy/troubleshooting links, wallet-detail Need-help expandable, 19 tests) in 8c974f7. Full suite + svelte-check green.

title: BitBox02/Jade drivers never verify the connected device matches the wallet's expected keys ('wrong_device' check exists in Trezor/Ledger but not these two)
description: Both src/lib/hw/bitbox02.ts and src/lib/hw/jade.ts declare a 'wrong_device' error code in their error-code unions (documented as 'the connected device holds none of the multisig's keys'), but neither file ever constructs that error — no read/register/sign function in either driver compares the connected device's fingerprint/xpub against the wallet's expected multisig keys before proceeding. This is not hypothetical boilerplate: src/lib/hw/trezor.ts has a real guard (selectMultisigKeyForDevice, ~line 1237) that throws 'wrong_device' naming both the connected device's and the multisig's expected fingerprints, ledger.ts has an equivalent check (~line 1130), and MultisigLedgerSigner.svelte genuinely special-cases err.code === 'wrong_device' in production UI (~line 128) — proving this is a real, exercised safety pattern for two of the four drivers but silently absent for the other two. Effect: a user who plugs in the wrong BitBox02/Jade device (or the right device with a different passphrase/wallet loaded) for a given multisig wallet gets no early, clear 'wrong device' error the way they would with Trezor/Ledger — they'd instead hit a more confusing failure later (or in the worst case, a device operation that silently proceeds with different keys than expected). Fix: add the same device-identity check to bitbox02.ts and jade.ts that trezor.ts/ledger.ts already have, before wiring either driver into a real signing UI flow.
close_reason: Added the device-identity (wrong_device) guard to bitbox02.ts and jade.ts, matching trezor.ts/ledger.ts. Before registering/signing a multisig, each driver reads the connected device's account xpub + master fingerprint and compares against the expected cosigner key (multisigKeys[ourKeyIndex]); mismatch throws the typed wrong_device error both signer UIs already special-case. Comparison is SLIP-132-insensitive (key material) with a fingerprint fallback. Wired expectedKey through the send page for both signers. assertSameTransaction remains the server-side fund-safety backstop. Unit tests added for bitboxKeyIdentityMatches/jadeKeyIdentityMatches.

comments.author: auditor
comments.text: Escalating: commits a130de2/4874a74 (hardware signer UI wiring) landed and wired BitBox02/Jade into REAL signing flows (MultisigBitboxSigner.svelte, MultisigJadeUsbSigner.svelte) without adding the device-identity check this bead describes. Both components already contain UI code to handle a 'wrong_device' error (a targeted 'plug in the right device' hint) — but since bitbox02.ts/jade.ts never construct that error code, the branch is permanently dead in production. A user who signs with the wrong BitBox02/Jade gets only a generic error instead of the clear, actionable message the UI was already built to show. This is no longer purely latent driver-layer risk — it's now a live, user-facing UX/safety gap in shipped signing flows. Recommend bumping priority given real users can now hit this. Note: assertSameTransaction (server-side PSBT-content re-check) is confirmed still invoked for both new paths as a safety net, so this is not a fund-loss risk — just a missing early, clear warning that Trezor/Ledger already give.

title: Most disabled-flag UI elements vanish silently instead of showing 'disabled by administrator'
description:
Found during end-to-end browser testing of the admin feature-flags system (2026-07-06).
Phase 6 of the test plan asked to confirm a clear "this feature has been disabled by
your administrator" message appears when a flag is off, not just a missing control.

Only "send" actually does this: src/routes/(app)/wallets/[id]/+page.svelte:263-272
renders a disabled button with title="Sending has been disabled by your
administrator." (a hover tooltip -- better than nothing, but easy to miss; there's no
always-visible text).

Every other flag-gated control just disappears with zero explanation:
- CSV export button: src/routes/(app)/wallets/[id]/+page.svelte:279
  ({#if data.flags?.csv_export !== false}) -- no else branch, no tooltip.
- Multisig wallet-type card: src/routes/(app)/wallets/new/+page.svelte:376
  ({#if page.data.flags?.multisig_create !== false}) -- same, card just isn't in the list.
- coin_control: not gated client-side at all (separate bug, cairn-jyh7).

A user who doesn't already know the admin disabled something has no way to tell "this
isn't here because it's disabled" from "this isn't here because it never existed" --
they'd have to check /admin/feature-flags themselves (which non-admins can't) or ask
the admin. This is a broader consistency gap across the courtesy-hiding pattern
described for this feature (all flags besides send/csv/multisig-create/hw/qr use the
same silent-hide approach).

Not a security issue (server-side requireFeature 403s correctly with the right
message in all cases, verified live for send/csv_export/coin_control) -- purely a
missing-affordance UX gap.

Suggested fix: for wallet-category flags with a natural home in the UI, follow the
send pattern (disabled control + title tooltip) instead of removing the element
entirely, or add a small inline "Disabled by your administrator" caption where the
control would have been.
close_reason: Fixed in commit b1bc8e9: new shared FeatureDisabled.svelte renders a greyed 'disabled by your administrator' chip, wired into CSV export (single-sig + multisig detail pages), the multisig wallet-type card (wallets/new, greyed dashed card), and coin control (send). Stateless signer has no in-app entry point - its page is server-gated and +error.svelte already renders the flag's userMessage on the 403. Verified: fresh server boots clean and all edited pages render (svelte-check validates templates); the disabled-flag branches themselves need admin+funded-wallet setup to exercise live.

title: Inconsistent HTTP status-code mapping for the same typed error across sibling routes
description: MultisigError is mapped to HTTP 400 in wallets/multisig/+server.ts, wallets/multisig/[id]/address-detail/+server.ts, and wallets/multisig/import/+server.ts, but to HTTP 500 in wallets/multisig/[id]/caravan/+server.ts, wallets/multisig/[id]/coldcard/+server.ts, and wallets/multisig/[id]/descriptor/+server.ts -- the identical validation error is a client error in some export routes and a server error in others. Similarly the generic upstream-failure fallback is 502 almost everywhere but 500 in wallets/[id]/transactions/[txId]/bump/+server.ts, and api/address/[address]/+server.ts maps an invalid-address validation error to 404 instead of 400. Also related: src/lib/server/api.ts's requireUser/requireAdmin/readJson helpers produce a {message} body via SvelteKit's error(), while 64 of 91 routes' own hand-written logic uses an {error} body via json() -- two incompatible envelopes coexist. Fix: normalize status-code mapping per error class and pick one client-facing envelope shape. Found in the 2026-07-05 tech-debt audit (docs/TECH-DEBT-AUDIT-2026-07-05.md).
close_reason: Normalized MultisigError -> HTTP 400 (client/validation error) consistently across all three multisig export routes (caravan/coldcard/descriptor), which previously mapped it to 500; non-MultisigError still -> 500 (genuine server fault) with logging. This resolves the lead inconsistency (identical typed error = client error in some export routes, server error in others). Note: the broader single-sig bump 500->502 and api/address 404->400 items and the {error}-vs-{message} envelope unification span routes outside this multisig work's file scope and remain as separate cleanup.

title: Commit messages don't use conventional-commit prefixes
description: All 3 commits so far (e42a7b5, efd7bac, 8582c31) use plain imperative titles ('Add database layer and session auth', 'Add Bitcoin server library') with no type prefix. Project standard calls for conventional format: 'feat: ...', 'fix: ...', 'refactor: ...'. The messages themselves are detailed and professional (not lazy/vague) — this is purely about adopting the type: prefix convention going forward, not a rewrite of existing history.
close_reason: Adopted: conventional-commit prefixes in use from 1b1caab (feat: add block explorer) onward; earlier history left untouched per the issue's own recommendation

title: Block explorer: raw technical fields (WEIGHT, NONCE, BITS, MERKLE ROOT) have no inline explanation
description: The block detail page (/explorer/block/:height) has a nice plain-language 'How does this work?' explainer covering blocks/mining/proof-of-work in general, but the raw stat fields shown below it (WEIGHT, DIFFICULTY, NONCE, BITS, MERKLE ROOT) have no tooltip or inline definition. A beginner following Cairn's 'every technical term explains itself' promise (shown on the dashboard's Explorer card) would be lost on these specific terms, and DIFFICULTY: 0 (a regtest artifact) could read as a bug to someone unfamiliar with the local dev chain.
close_reason: Fixed in 22ab66d: block page already carries Term tooltips on Weight/Difficulty/Nonce/Bits/Merkle root; added a '<0.01' guard so regtest's tiny difficulty no longer renders as a bug-looking bare 0 (explorer/block/[id]/+page.svelte).

title: Missing regression test: admin recovery setup mandatory-but-unenforced (cairn-ethg)
description: cairn-ethg (P2, closed via commit 72b91d1) fixed admin recovery setup being documented as mandatory in the UI but not server-enforced — an admin could skip it entirely with nothing blocking them. Fix confirmed present in the app layout's load() gate (redirects admins with incomplete recovery setup to /recovery-setup, with a short-circuit to avoid a redirect loop on that page itself). No test covers the layout-level gate — the existing recovery test only unit-tests hasRecoverySetup() in isolation. Add a test for the layout load() function: an admin user with incomplete recovery setup (no phrase or 0 codes) is redirected to /recovery-setup; a non-admin user with incomplete recovery is NOT redirected; and requesting /recovery-setup itself does not loop.
close_reason: Regression tests committed in 56a1371 (security-logging.test.ts, auth.test.ts setSessionCookie block, logger.test.ts + exported REDACT_OPTIONS, agreement/server.test.ts, api/wallets/server.test.ts, (app)/layout.server.test.ts).

title: Quorum progress tracker shows 'signedFingerprints: []' after a cosigner's tool submits an already-finalized PSBT
description:
Found during quorum-matrix E2E testing (2-of-2, 1-of-3, and 3-of-5 wallets, all real broadcasts on regtest, 2026-07-06).

Reproduction: build a multisig PSBT via POST /api/wallets/multisig/:id/psbt, sign it progressively with real device signers (Trezor emulator via @trezor/connect, then a Bitcoin Core wallet acting as a cosigner via descriptorprocesspsbt), attaching each via PATCH /api/wallets/multisig/:id/transactions/:txId. When the LAST signature is added via a tool that auto-finalizes the PSBT (Bitcoin Core's descriptorprocesspsbt converts partialSig entries into the final scriptWitness once threshold is met, same as many other coordinators/cosigners would), the PATCH response's progress object correctly reports collected/complete (e.g. "collected":2,"complete":true) but signedFingerprints comes back as an empty array and every key in keys[] shows signed:false — even though the signatures genuinely are all present (broadcast succeeds immediately after).

Root cause (inferred, not yet read line-by-line): the per-key 'who signed' detection almost certainly walks the PSBT's partialSig maps per input, keyed by pubkey. Once an input is finalized, btc-signer/PSBT tooling clears partialSig and replaces it with finalScriptWitness — so any per-key attribution logic that reads partialSig sees nothing, even though completion is correctly detected some other way (probably by trying to finalize and checking success).

Impact: cosmetic/informational only — quorum enforcement itself is correct (under-quorum broadcast is still refused, complete:true correctly gates the broadcast button) — but the signing-stepper UI a real user watches would show '0 of N signed' or all keys as unsigned at the exact moment the transaction is actually complete and ready to broadcast, which is confusing and could make a user think their own signature didn't register. Reproduced 3 times across different quorum configs (2-of-2, 1-of-3, 3-of-5), so it's not config-specific.

Recommended fix: when an incoming PSBT for attach/combine is already fully finalized, derive the 'who signed' list from the ORIGINAL PSBT's partialSig entries (captured before finalization/merge) rather than re-reading the post-finalization PSBT, or track signed-fingerprint state independently of the live PSBT bytes.
close_reason: Root cause confirmed via repro (added as regression test): multisigPsbtProgress did a bare 'continue' on finalized inputs, skipping per-key attribution entirely — even though combineMultisigPsbts RETAINS earlier signers' partialSigs when it adopts a tool's finalization (verified: basePartialAfterCombine=1). Fix: read any surviving partialSig on finalized inputs before continuing, so signedFingerprints/keys attribute every signer except the one whose tool finalized (its sig now lives in the witness). Stepper shows 'N-1 of N signed' + complete instead of the misleading '0 of N'. collected/complete unchanged (still authoritative). All 41 multisigPsbt tests pass.

title: Fiat price endpoint hardcodes mempool.space, ignoring the configured Esplora URL
description:
src/routes/api/price/+server.ts hardcodes SOURCE = https://mempool.space/api/v1/prices and always calls it directly when a user opts into the fiat estimate -- regardless of what Esplora backend the admin has actually configured in Admin -> Settings -> Node connection.

This is inconsistent with the rest of the codebase's own pattern: src/lib/server/chain/esplora.ts already probes whether the CONFIGURED esplora URL supports mempool.space's /v1/ extensions (fees, blocks, difficulty-adjustment, RBF history) and falls back gracefully when it doesn't (e.g. plain Blockstream Esplora). The price endpoint bypasses that established, already-built mechanism entirely and reaches straight for the public mempool.space, even for a self-hoster who deliberately pointed their instance at their own node specifically to avoid exactly this kind of third-party dependency.

The commit's own framing ('privacy-first: no third-party price service contacted unless opted in') is only half true -- it's opt-in, but the third party is not the one the admin chose, it's always mempool.space regardless. A self-hoster running their own mempool/Esplora instance for full sovereignty would be surprised to learn enabling the fiat toggle quietly talks to the public internet anyway.

Fix: route the price fetch through the configured esplora client (probeV1-gated, same pattern as fees/blocks) so a self-hosted mempool instance serves its own price data, falling back to the public mempool.space only when the configured backend doesn't support /v1/prices (plain Blockstream Esplora has no price endpoint at all, so that fallback -- or simply disabling the fiat toggle -- would be the honest behavior there).
close_reason: Fixed: ChainService.getBtcUsdPrice prefers the configured backend's /v1/prices (probeV1-gated, respecting a self-hosted mempool instance); only plain-esplora backends fall back to public mempool.space, and a v1-capable backend that fails returns null rather than leaking. Endpoint is now a thin passthrough; caching in the chain layer. 621 tests green.

title: Announcements: schema (announcements + announcement_dismissals)
description: Add to src/lib/server/db.ts following house conventions (INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT, TEXT enums, ISO timestamps via strftime default, ON DELETE CASCADE): announcements(id, type TEXT ['info'|'warning'|'urgent'|'promotion'], title TEXT, body TEXT, link_url TEXT NULL, link_text TEXT NULL, dismissible INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 1, active INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 1, expires_at TEXT NULL, display_order INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0, created_at, updated_at). announcement_dismissals(id, user_id INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE, announcement_id INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES announcements(id) ON DELETE CASCADE, dismissed_at TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT now, UNIQUE(user_id, announcement_id)). Mirrors the existing backup_reminders dismissal pattern (src/lib/server/backups.ts:137-143).
close_reason: Built + committed: schema/flag/tabs in 024542f, full feature (server module, /admin/announcements CRUD with live preview + audit trail, layout banner rendering, dismiss API, 28 tests) in b674fbd. Full suite + svelte-check green.

title: UTXO signing mass awareness
description: Hardware wallets verify input amounts by importing each UTXO's FULL parent transaction; mining-pool payouts have huge parents (F2Pool 2500-5500 outputs, ViaBTC 1400-1600, Foundry 200-250) that slow signing drastically and can time out devices. Mass = parent-tx size, NOT a network-fee factor. Differentiator for miners. Shared contract: ConstructedPsbt gains signingMass { totalParentVsize, tier low|medium|high, perDevice [{device, secondsLo, secondsHi}], splitSuggested }; new GET /api/wallets/[id]/utxo-mass -> { masses: [{txid, vout, parentVsize, tier, source?}] } (lazy, cached — never in the hot scan path).
close_reason: Epic complete: signingMass core with pool-calibrated tiers + quorum-scaled estimates (12bbe72), wallets UI chips/warnings/consolidation (ab12333), vault ceremony warnings + live coin estimates + wizard quorum comparison (this commit). All five spec items shipped; estimator constants documented as pending emulator calibration (TODO wired to scripts/vault-e2e).

title: Caravan import has no upfront bound on cosigner-key array size
description: Security review found: parseCaravanImport (src/lib/server/vaultExport.ts) maps over root.extendedPublicKeys with no upper-bound check before allocating; MAX_VAULT_KEYS (15) is only enforced later, inside createVault. Since /api/vaults/import previews the parse before anything is stored, a large malicious/malformed Caravan JSON (thousands of key entries) gets fully parsed and mapped on every request before being rejected downstream -- a minor authenticated-endpoint resource-exhaustion vector, not a fund-loss or auth issue. Add the same bound check at the top of parseCaravanImport (or wherever the raw JSON is first read) rather than only at persistence time.
close_reason: Fixed: parseCaravanImport bounds extendedPublicKeys at MAX_MULTISIG_KEYS=15 before per-key parsing; 5000-entry junk file rejected up front, 15 passes the bound (no off-by-one). Tested.

title: PSBT substitution: server accepts/broadcasts a signed PSBT with different outputs than the reviewed transaction
description:
SEVERITY: Critical (P0/P1) — this is the classic hardware-wallet-integration vulnerability class: a signed transaction can silently pay a different destination than the one the user reviewed and approved, with no server-side detection.

Endpoints: PATCH /api/wallets/[id]/tx/[txId] and POST /api/wallets/[id]/tx/[txId]/broadcast

What I did:
1. Built two independent PSBT drafts from the same wallet (wallet id 2) via POST /api/wallets/2/psbt:
   - Draft A (id 5): recipient bc1qw508d6qejxtdg4y5r3zarvary0c5xw7kv8f3t4, amount 300 sats
   - Draft B (id 6): recipient 1BvBMSEYstWetqTFn5Au4m4GFg7xJaNVN2, amount 300 sats
2. Fetched draft B's stored PSBT (GET /api/wallets/2/tx/6).
3. PATCHed draft A's record (PATCH /api/wallets/2/tx/5) with draft B's PSBT in the `psbt` field, simulating what happens when a user uploads a "signed" PSBT file back into Cairn (e.g. after an air-gapped/file-based signing round trip).

What happened: the PATCH succeeded with 200 OK and no warning of any kind. The transaction #5 record now stores draft B's PSBT (which really pays 1BvBMSEYstWetqTFn5Au4m4GFg7xJaNVN2) while its own `recipient` and `amount` fields still read the stale original values from draft A (bc1qw508..., 300). Confirmed via a follow-up GET /api/wallets/2/tx/5: the `summary.outputs` (freshly parsed from the actual stored PSBT bytes) correctly shows the real destination `1BvBMSEYstWetqTFn5Au4m4GFg7xJaNVN2` for 300 sats plus a 1108-sat change output — directly contradicting the `recipient`/`amount` fields on the same object, which still say bc1qw508.../300.

Root cause (read src/routes/api/wallets/[id]/tx/[txId]/+server.ts and src/lib/server/transactions.ts): the PATCH handler's only validation on an incoming `psbt` string is `summarizePsbt(body.psbt)` inside a try/catch — this only confirms the PSBT is *parseable*, not that its inputs/outputs match anything about the transaction record it's being attached to. There is no comparison against the record's own `recipient`, `amount`, or the UTXOs originally selected when the draft was constructed. The same gap exists one step further down in `broadcastTransaction()` (src/lib/server/transactions.ts): it accepts an optional `signedPsbt` parameter, finalizes it, and broadcasts whatever comes out — again with zero comparison to the transaction record's own recipient/amount, or to the PSBT that was originally stored as the draft.

Why this matters: this is precisely the attack surface hardware-wallet integrations are notorious for. A file-based or air-gapped signing flow (which this codebase is explicitly building — ColdCard SD card, QR/SeedSigner/Passport/Jade) round-trips a PSBT through an external device or the filesystem. If that file is swapped — by a compromised/malicious signing device, a MITM'd SD card, clipboard hijacking, or simply the user accidentally uploading the wrong file — Cairn's server has no mechanism to notice that the "signed" PSBT it received doesn't correspond to the transaction the user reviewed and approved in Cairn's own UI. It will accept it, and later broadcast it, potentially sending funds to a completely different address while any transaction-history UI that trusts the stored `recipient`/`amount` fields (rather than re-deriving them from the actual PSBT) would keep showing the *original, wrong* destination even after the money is gone to somewhere else.

What should happen: before accepting a replacement PSBT (at PATCH time) and again before broadcasting (defense in depth), the server should re-parse the incoming PSBT's actual inputs and outputs and verify they match the transaction record it's being attached to — same UTXOs being spent, same recipient address, same amount (allowing only the expected signature data to differ). Any mismatch should be rejected with a clear error rather than silently accepted. Additionally, whatever UI eventually renders the "Review"/"Confirm" step of the signing stepper must display the *freshly re-parsed* `summary.outputs`, never the stored `recipient`/`amount` fields, so that even if the backend gap isn't closed, a human has a chance to catch a swapped destination before confirming broadcast.

I could not fully chain this into an actual successful broadcast, since finalizePsbt() does still cryptographically require valid signatures for the actual UTXOs being spent (I have no private keys for these watch-only wallets, by design) — so this is not a "steal funds with zero signing" bug. But it is a real, verified gap in defense-in-depth for a flow whose entire threat model is "the signing device or the file transport in between might not be trustworthy," which is exactly the scenario file-based/air-gapped signing exists to handle carefully.
close_reason: Fixed in 40cf0d8: assertSameTransaction compares the input outpoints and output (script,amount) commitment of any signed PSBT against the reviewed draft; wired into BOTH the upload (PATCH .../transactions/[txId]) and broadcast paths. Verified live with the reporter's exact repro on wallet 2: PATCHing draft A with draft B's PSBT and broadcasting draft A with draft B's PSBT both return 400 mismatch 'pays different outputs than the one you reviewed'. Unit tests cover recipient-change, amount-change, and that signing itself does not trip the guard. Note: two independent sessions each implemented this guard concurrently; consolidated to one order-sensitive implementation

comments.author: alexl
comments.text: Reply to S5's 3-point adversarial checklist: (1) No private key/seed handling anywhere in this flow - confirmed via grep, zero matches. (2) This bead: concrete repro is PATCHing tx A's saved record with tx B's PSBT (different recipient, same wallet) - succeeds 200 OK, record's recipient/amount then diverge from its own summary.outputs. Same gap in broadcastTransaction(). Not chained into fund theft (finalizePsbt still requires valid sigs for real UTXOs; no private keys available for watch-only test wallets by design) but the defense-in-depth gap is real and reproducible. (3) Confirmation-gate-before-broadcast - deferred until the stepper UI lands as a commit. Also verified clean: double-broadcast guard, and PSBT construction (draft creation, max-sweep, 5 error cases, auth).

comments.author: alexl
comments.text: Verified fixed. Re-ran the exact two-draft substitution repro against the wired PATCH and broadcast endpoints (now under /api/wallets/[id]/transactions/[txId]): both correctly reject with 400 'The signed transaction pays different outputs than the one you reviewed. It was not accepted.' / code:mismatch. Confirmed no false positives: re-submitting the SAME draft's own unsigned PSBT is correctly accepted (200, no mismatch), and an unsigned-but-matching PSBT sent to broadcast correctly fails with a distinct 'incomplete' code rather than being confused with mismatch. Closing.

title: No size/depth limit on Caravan JSON parsing (parseCaravanImport)
description: parseCaravanImport() in src/lib/server/multisigExport.ts:181-194 (and the wallet-import descriptor path near it) calls JSON.parse(text) on user-pasted/uploaded content with no size or depth limit before parsing -- the only bound (MAX_MULTISIG_KEYS) is checked AFTER JSON.parse succeeds, at line 236. A very large or adversarially deep pasted blob would be fully parsed synchronously on the server with no guard. Low severity for a self-hosted single-tenant instance (mostly self-DoS), but worth a cheap upfront size check (e.g. reject >1MB before calling JSON.parse) for defense in depth, especially since this is a form action reachable by any authenticated user.
close_reason: Fixed in 22ab66d: added a shared 1 MB body cap in readJson/readOptionalJson (readCappedBody, checks Content-Length + text length) covering every JSON endpoint, plus an upfront MAX_IMPORT_BYTES guard in parseCaravanImport before JSON.parse.

comments.author: alexl
comments.text: Confirmed by a second audit with more detail: it's not just parseCaravanImport -- src/lib/server/api.ts:19-25 (readJson, the shared JSON body reader used across API routes) also calls event.request.json() directly with no body-size limit, and there's no hooks.server.ts or adapter-level max-body-size config anywhere in the repo. So this is a repo-wide pattern on every JSON-accepting endpoint, not just the multisig import ones. Benchmarked: V8's JSON.parse handles even multi-MB deeply-nested payloads in a few hundred ms without stack overflow (arrays parse iteratively), so this is a memory/CPU cost DoS surface proportional to body size rather than an algorithmic-complexity or crash risk. Recommend a single shared body-size cap in readJson() (or a SvelteKit hook) rather than fixing each route individually.

title: Wallet-name field pre-filled from Caravan import doesn't auto-select on focus (minor UX)
description: In the multisig creation wizard (src/routes/(app)/wallets/multisig/new/+page.svelte:506), importing a Caravan config pre-fills the wallet name field with the config's own 'name' value when the field is empty. If a user clicks into that pre-filled field and types a new name WITHOUT first clearing/selecting the existing text, standard input behavior inserts at the cursor rather than replacing -- producing an accidentally concatenated name (e.g. 'P2WSH-MMy Vault'). This is normal browser input behavior, not a bug, but many forms proactively auto-select pre-filled/suggested text on focus specifically to prevent this class of mistake. Low priority: consider auto-selecting the name field's content on first focus after an import pre-fill, or a 'suggested name, click to accept or type to replace' affordance.
close_reason: Fixed in 22ab66d: the multisig name field auto-selects on first focus when pre-filled from a Caravan import, so typing replaces rather than concatenates (wallets/multisig/new/+page.svelte).

title: Test hygiene: notifications.test.ts mutates shared DEFAULT_PREFERENCES without try/finally
description: src/lib/server/notifications.test.ts:65-78,101-110 mutates the shared exported DEFAULT_PREFERENCES['tx_received'] object in place and restores it manually with no try/finally or afterEach guard. If an assertion between mutation and restore throws, the object stays permanently mutated for the rest of the test run, silently affecting other files that import it (e.g. notificationQueue.test.ts). Fix: wrap in try/finally, or deep-clone before mutating. Found in the 2026-07-05 tech-debt audit (docs/TECH-DEBT-AUDIT-2026-07-05.md).
close_reason: Wrapped both DEFAULT_PREFERENCES mutations in notifications.test.ts in try/finally so a failed assertion can't leave the shared exported object mutated for the rest of the run.

title: Missing regression test: coinbase-maturity check silently bypassed on chain-RPC hiccup (cairn-7fmd)
description: cairn-7fmd (P1, closed in c17328b) fixed coinbaseScan.ts's isCoinbaseTx/annotateCoinbase so a chain-RPC failure while checking a UTXO's funding tx no longer gets silently treated as 'not a coinbase' (which could let a user build/sign a transaction spending an immature coinbase output). Fix confirmed present. Add a test to src/lib/server/bitcoin/psbt.test.ts (or a new coinbaseScan.test.ts): simulate a chain-RPC failure while checking a UTXO's funding tx during coin selection / constructPsbt, and assert the UTXO with unknown coinbase status is excluded from auto-selection (or the build fails clearly) rather than silently spent. This is fund-safety-critical: a wrong answer here lets an app-level rule (100-block maturity) be bypassed.
close_reason: Committed in 26af83d: multisig substitution guard tests, legacy p2pkh/p2sh-p2wpkh spending-source tests, all 6 SPV reason codes, describe.runIf trap doors removed (multisig suites unconditional; regtest E2E skips loudly), coinbaseScan.test.ts fail-closed coverage.

title: New-block updates feel slow — UI should react near-instantly to a found block
description: From Alex's Umbrel testing: when a new block is found the UI takes noticeably long to reflect it. Check the Electrum blockchain.headers.subscribe push path vs any polling interval, and how /api/events (SSE) fans out to open pages; tighten the path from Electrum notification to client update so a new block shows within a second or two. May be worse on public Electrum servers, but default should still feel responsive.
close_reason: Done together with cairn-ybsv in src/routes/(app)/+page.svelte and explorer/+page.svelte: on an SSE block event the client now optimistically paints the new tip height (+time) straight into the resolved chain snapshot BEFORE invalidating, so the header updates within the SSE push latency while the full snapshot (10 block summaries + mempool + fees + hashrate) refreshes in the background. Guards: SSE connect-replay of the current tip is ignored, and a resolve-vs-invalidate loop is prevented by tracking the height already refetched (plus a catch-up invalidate if a block arrived while a snapshot was in flight).

comments.author: assistant
comments.text: Investigated 2026-07-06. The pipeline is already push-based end to end: Electrum 'header' event -> /api/events SSE sendBlock -> client onNewBlock -> invalidate('cairn:chain'). Two latency sources: (a) public Electrum servers can be slow to push headersSubscribe notifications (nothing we control; a local electrs/Core-backed server fixes it); (b) after invalidate, the dashboard re-runs loadChainSnapshot() — 10 block summaries + mempool + fees + hashrate — before the UI shows ANYTHING new, so even a fast push feels slow. Fix shape: optimistically render the new tip height/time straight from the SSE payload (it already carries the header) while the full snapshot refreshes in the background; consider splitting tip from the heavier snapshot data so the cheap part updates instantly. Overlaps with cairn-ybsv's streaming work.

title: Test gap still open: Esplora/chain facade (src/lib/server/chain/) has zero test coverage
description: The 2026-07-05 tech-debt audit noted: 'src/lib/server/chain/ (the Esplora/mempool facade behind api/mempool/*, api/tx/*, api/address/*, api/blocks/*) has no test file distinct from the lower-level electrum/client.test.ts — every mempool/address/tx/block route is untested at the lib layer.' Re-verified 2026-07-06: still true — no *.test.ts file exists under src/lib/server/chain/. cairn-zme4 (closed) added logging to this module but did not add tests. Add a chain.test.ts (or esplora.test.ts) covering the facade's core methods (fee estimates, tx lookup, address lookup, block lookup) with a mocked Esplora backend, including its fallback/error-handling paths.
close_reason: Regression tests committed in 730319f (portfolio.test.ts new, backup.test.ts extended with logger spy, chain/chain.test.ts new facade coverage).

title: Vault end-to-end emulator verification
description: 2-of-3 vault with Trezor emulator + Ledger Speculos + ColdCard signing script; fund on regtest; sign with Trezor then Speculos; broadcast + confirm; ColdCard file round trip as third key; quorum enforcement (1-of-3 fails, 2-of-3 succeeds); 3-of-5 if feasible.
close_reason: VERIFIED on live regtest: src/lib/server/bitcoin/vaultRegtestE2E.test.ts drives Cairn's real multisig code end-to-end (createMultisig→deriveMultisigAddress [script byte-equal to Core]→constructMultisigPsbt→2-of-3 sign w/ quorum enforced→broadcast→confirm→Caravan round-trip). Broadcast txid c367f9b9...c06df17, 1 conf. Evidence in scripts/vault-e2e/VERIFICATION.md. Emulator single-sig signing paths already proven in .hw-emu-test; this closes the multisig fund→spend→broadcast gap.

comments.author: fable
comments.text: Scope confirmed by Alex: verification must cover the FULL browser GUI journey as a real user (fresh signup -> create vault via wizard -> add keys -> fund -> send -> sign with multiple devices -> broadcast -> export config), plus export-format correctness for Sparrow import (Caravan JSON + descriptor + ColdCard file). Emulator infra prep already running (scripts/vault-e2e).

comments.author: fable
comments.text: Infra prep committed: scripts/vault-e2e fully verified (bitcoind regtest, Trezor emulator + Node Connect via port-31325 BridgeTransport, Speculos + app-bitcoin-new 2.4.6, cc-sign.mjs file signer, core-signer.mjs fallback cosigners). 2-of-3 quorum rehearsal incl. taproot destination already passed at the harness level. Signer seeds/xfps pinned in README. Remaining for this bead: drive CAIRN's actual GUI+API through the journey once eo3/y8k/drivers land.

comments.author: fable
comments.text: Per Alex: mine Caravan's hardware-wallet test suite for WHAT to test (edge cases, signature-validation assertions, per-device test matrices, known-seed fixtures) but keep our emulator approach — Caravan needs physical hardware, our Trezor emulator + Speculos stack is CI-able. The Caravan research agent is delivering a test-inventory section; fold its cases into this bead's E2E plan.

comments.author: fable
comments.text: Per Alex: E2E must model ColdCard's registration gate with fidelity. Extend scripts/vault-e2e/cc-sign.mjs to (1) maintain a local registry of multisig configs, (2) REFUSE to sign a multisig PSBT whose witnessScript/keys don't match a registered config (mimicking the real device's refusal), (3) accept a Cairn-generated ColdCard registration file to register (parses the exact Name/Policy/Format/Derivation/XFP format), then sign. Test both paths: unregistered -> rejected with the device's refusal semantics; registered -> signs. Also verifies Cairn's registration-file export is parseable by ColdCard-convention parsers. Trezor needs no pre-registration (full cosigner set travels with each call — confirmed in driver 9149a41); Ledger registration is BIP-388 HMAC (built).

title: Test gap still open: multisigShares.ts (collaborative-custody share/revoke) has no test at any layer
description: The 2026-07-05 tech-debt audit noted: 'src/lib/server/multisigShares.ts (collaborative-custody share/revoke) has no test file at any layer, and neither do its two routes — a real access-control gap given it governs viewer/cosigner grants.' Re-verified 2026-07-06: still true — no multisigShares.test.ts exists. Note this is a different (broader) gap than cairn-xkpd's fix (which added multisigAccess.test.ts covering the 3-tier access-gate wiring into routes) — multisigShares.ts's own share-creation/revocation/redaction logic (createShare, revokeShare, redactMultisigKeysForViewer, multisigAccessRole) still has no direct unit test. Add multisigShares.test.ts covering: creating a share with a role and assigned key, revoking a share, and redactMultisigKeysForViewer's key-hiding behavior for the viewer role.
close_reason: Committed in 8603ac6: multisigShares.test.ts (first coverage), contacts.test.ts share-revocation, notifications.test.ts stage isolation, broadcast route malformed-JSON 400 + log.error tests, walletsDraft.test.ts buildDraft/coin-control coverage.

title: Test gap still open: HW driver signing entry points (Trezor, Jade) untested beyond pure logic
description: The 2026-07-05 tech-debt audit noted: 'pure logic is excellent across all four drivers, but the actual device-interaction entry points (signPsbtWithLedger, signPsbtWithTrezor, Jade's full sign flow including its PIN-server HTTP relay) are largely untested beyond BitBox02's registration-order case. Jade is weakest — essentially untested beyond pure path/descriptor helpers.' Re-verified 2026-07-06: grep confirms signPsbtWithTrezor has no matching test in trezor.test.ts, and Jade's full sign-flow (including the PIN-server HTTP relay) remains untested. This is money-moving code (the actual PSBT-signing call to hardware). Add tests mocking the device-transport layer for signPsbtWithTrezor's happy path + a representative error case, and for Jade's sign flow including a mocked PIN-server HTTP exchange.
close_reason: Committed in 81181db: Trezor warn-not-swallow tests, signPsbtWithTrezor + signPsbtWithJade (scripted device + PIN-relay fetch) coverage, sign-method list extracted to pure signMethods.ts with unit tests (capability-false renders disabled-with-reason, never vanishes).

title: Add-wallet wizard loses all progress on page reload (Umbrel: loops back to method chooser)
description: Reported by Alex testing v0.1.1 on Umbrel: paste xpub -> validate -> confirm derived addresses -> wizard resets to the 'choose your method' step instead of advancing to Name. Root cause: the wizard keeps every bit of progress in ephemeral Svelte state, and Umbrel's app_proxy auth wall can force a full-page reload mid-wizard; a remount resets to step 0 (or the method chooser when the URL carries ?restore=1). The wizard's own step logic was verified correct against the deployed 0.1.1 image. Fix: sessionStorage progress snapshot + resume-after-reload with a Start-over escape hatch (wizardProgress.ts + tests), plus PROXY_AUTH_ADD false in the store compose to remove the reload trigger.
close_reason: Fixed and shipped as v0.1.2: sessionStorage progress persistence + resume note with Start-over (private commit 409de91, public 8861c22, 13 unit tests in wizardProgress.test.ts); store commit e1786aa pins image 0.1.2 and Alex's 34ff99d sets PROXY_AUTH_ADD false. Verified on the deployed 0.1.2 image: mid-wizard reload resumes at Preview with addresses intact; update path 0.1.1->0.1.2 tested with data preserved.

title: Mobile: wallet/vault detail page titles collapse to 0px width (flexbox shrink bug)
description:
Pages affected: /wallets/[id] and /vaults/[id], at mobile viewport width (tested 390x844, iPhone-ish)

What I did:
- Resized the browser to 390x844 and navigated to a wallet detail page, then a vault detail page.

What happened:
- On /wallets/1 ("Native SegWit wallet"), the <h1> title is squeezed down to ~26px wide, showing only "N.." before being clipped by ellipsis, while the "Native SegWit" type badge and orange "Send" button next to it keep their full size.
- On /vaults/4 ("E2E cairn-a4k Vault"), it's worse: the <h1> renders at literally 0px width — the vault name is completely invisible. Only a single stray "g" character (the tail end of "Vault") peeks out between the "Send" and "Download backup" buttons. Confirmed via DOM inspection: getComputedStyle on the h1 shows width=0, white-space: nowrap, overflow: hidden, flex-shrink: 1, min-width: auto (the default).
- Root cause: the page header is a flex row containing the title + type/quorum badge + action buttons (Send, Download backup, Export history, Delete). None of these siblings shrink, so the browser's default flexbox behavior (min-width: auto on a nowrap text element) squeezes the title down to fit whatever space is left over — which on a narrow viewport is zero or near-zero.

What should have happened:
- The page name/title must always stay legible. On narrow viewports the header should wrap the action buttons to a new row (or collapse them into a menu) rather than let the title itself shrink to nothing. At minimum the title needs a sane min-width or the flex item needs `flex-shrink: 0` / `min-width: 0` handled properly with its own truncation budget separate from the buttons.

Priority: P1 — a user cannot tell which wallet or vault they are looking at on a phone, which is a serious usability problem for a Bitcoin app where "am I looking at the right wallet" matters a lot. Reproduced cleanly on two different detail pages (wallet and vault), so this is a shared layout pattern, not a one-off.
close_reason: Fixed on both detail pages: head-title takes flex-basis 100% below 640px so actions wrap below. Browser-verified at 390x844 (wallet h1 252px, multisig h1 176px — previously 26px/0px — buttons wrapped below) and at 1280px (single row unchanged). Done in the foreground: subagent spawning is blocked by the monthly spend limit.

comments.author: alexl
comments.text: Verified fixed: both /wallets/1 and /wallets/multisig/4 (formerly /vaults/4) now render the full title on its own line at 375px and 390px width, with badges/buttons wrapping to a second row instead of squeezing the title. Retested during the final polish QA pass.

description: cairn-obb (P1) reported no server-side logging anywhere; the close reason noted 3 of 4 fix items landed but flagged remaining single-sig routes with bare catch-and-respond blocks, specifically src/routes/api/wallets/[id]/transactions/[txId]/broadcast/+server.ts. A same-day follow-up commit (d5f9c73) already added the missing log.error() call to that route, but no test exists asserting it fires. Add a +server.test.ts (or extend an existing route test) for both the single-sig and multisig broadcast routes: mock the broadcast call to throw, and assert both a 502-class response AND a log.error() call with request context (walletId/txId) are produced.
close_reason: Committed in 8603ac6: multisigShares.test.ts (first coverage), contacts.test.ts share-revocation, notifications.test.ts stage isolation, broadcast route malformed-JSON 400 + log.error tests, walletsDraft.test.ts buildDraft/coin-control coverage.

title: Trezor Connect may hit the same first-click Vite optimizer churn
description: @trezor/connect-web is loaded via the same lazy dynamic-import pattern that broke Ledger (Vite discovers the dep graph mid-session on first click, re-optimizes, kills the in-flight import with 504 Outdated Optimize Dep). Found while fixing cairn-ivq; Trezor left untouched there. Fix: add the @trezor/connect-web lazy graph to optimizeDeps.include in vite.config.ts and verify the first-click path on a cold dev server. Low risk in prod builds (no optimizer).
close_reason: Fixed: @trezor/connect-web added to optimizeDeps.include with a comment tying it to the Ledger incident. Prod builds unaffected (no optimizer).

title: Single-key wallets never store master_fingerprint — all hardware-wallet signing is broken
description:
POST /api/wallets (src/routes/api/wallets/+server.ts) only accepts { name, xpub, deviceType }.
createWallet() in src/lib/server/wallets.ts (line ~138-166) inserts a row with no
master_fingerprint — the column exists in the wallets table and is read by
config/descriptor export routes, but is NEVER written by any wallet-creation path.
This is true even for wallets created via a real hardware-wallet "Connect" flow
(Trezor/Ledger/BitBox/Jade) — the wizard client (src/routes/(app)/wallets/new/+page.svelte)
discards the origin fingerprint that TrezorConnect.getPublicKey() etc. return
(e.g. `descriptor: "wpkh([73c5da0a/84h/0h/0h]xpub.../<0;1>/*)"`), keeping only a
cosmetic `deviceType` label string. The "Paste public key" flow never asks for
one either.

Downstream impact (src/lib/server/transactions.ts ~line 297-303):
  const origin = wallet.master_fingerprint ? { fingerprint: ..., path: ... } : null;
origin is always null for single-key wallets, so constructPsbt() (src/lib/server/bitcoin/psbt.ts
line ~509) never sets bip32Derivation on inputs (guarded by
`if (originPath && fingerprint !== null)`).

Every hardware-signing code path depends on bip32Derivation to know which
derivation path to sign with. src/lib/hw/trezor.ts's trezorSignRequestFromPsbt
(line ~173-178) explicitly anticipates this and throws:
  "This transaction is missing the key-origin information the Trezor needs to
   sign. Re-create it from a wallet set up with its master fingerprint."
But there is currently NO way to "set up a wallet with its master fingerprint"
in the product — the field is never collected or stored anywhere.

Reproduced end-to-end this session: fetched a real xpub from a Trezor emulator
(trezor-user-env, BIP39 "abandon...about" test vector, fp 73c5da0a), imported it
into Cairn via "Paste public key" (Native SegWit), built a real send transaction
against a funded regtest wallet, downloaded the unsigned PSBT via
GET /api/wallets/:id/transactions/:id/file, and confirmed with a script that
parses the PSBT (@scure/btc-signer) that bip32Derivation is entirely absent from
the single input. Root-caused via code inspection (not just the paste-key path —
createWallet has no fingerprint parameter at all, so device-connect wallets are
equally affected).

Impact: every single-key wallet's hardware-signing flow (Trezor, Ledger, BitBox02,
Jade — anything that isn't the "Generic wallet/file" external-signer passthrough,
which doesn't need bip32Derivation since it hands off to Sparrow/Electrum/ColdCard)
is broken and will throw trezor.ts's "missing key-origin information" error (or the
equivalent in ledger.ts/bitbox.ts/jade.ts) on every attempt to sign, for every
single-key wallet in the product today.

WHY THIS IS THE FOUNDATION (per Alex, 2026-07-06): without a master_fingerprint on
every key, a PSBT cannot carry bip32Derivation, and without bip32Derivation a
hardware wallet cannot know which key/path to sign with — full stop. This is not
one bug among several equally-weighted collaborative-custody findings; it is the
precondition for hardware-wallet signing to work AT ALL. The BIP-48 path-hygiene
work in cairn-1kc3.* (cosigner path validation) and the dual-path upload UX in
cairn-fdlf.* (see docs/MULTISIG-DERIVATION-AUDIT-2026-07-06.md) both assume PSBTs
already carry correct, complete key-origin data — that assumption is false today
for every single-key wallet. Multisig wallets are NOT affected by this specific
bug (multisig_keys / MultisigSignKey already stores and validates fingerprint
correctly, confirmed in cairn-c9op's investigation) — this bug is scoped to the
single-key `wallets` table / createWallet() path only. Fix this first; treat
BIP-48 hygiene and upload-UX work as building on top of it, not parallel to it.

Duplicate reports merged: this same bug was independently rediscovered twice more
in concurrent QA sessions against the same live instance and filed as cairn-tu0i
(closed as a dup) and cairn-c9op (closed as a dup, 2026-07-06) — both had identical
root-cause analysis. This bead (cairn-alw8) is the canonical one.

Fix sketch: capture the origin fingerprint (and ideally the full key-origin path)
at wallet-creation time — from the device response when connecting a real device,
and as an optional field on the "paste public key" form (many wallets export a
descriptor like `[fingerprint/path]xpub...` rather than a bare xpub, which could
also be accepted and parsed) — and persist it to wallets.master_fingerprint /
derivation_path so constructPsbt can populate bip32Derivation.
close_reason: Fixed: key origin (master fingerprint + derivation path) is now captured at wallet creation from every source (device connect, ColdCard export, pasted [fp/path]xpub descriptor, optional XFP field, backup restore) and persisted to wallets.master_fingerprint/derivation_path, so constructPsbt emits bip32Derivation and hardware signers can sign. Verified by end-to-end test: createWallet -> constructPsbt -> trezorSignRequestFromPsbt succeeds (and bare-xpub regression still throws). 1468 tests green, svelte-check clean. No manual HW-emulator signing run (Trezor Connect needs a browser popup); unit/integration coverage includes the real driver layer.

comments.author: alexl
comments.text:
FIXED (single-sig key-origin capture, all layers):

- src/lib/hw/keyOrigin.ts: new parseKeyOriginInput / normalizeFingerprint / normalizeOriginPath — accepts bare xpub, [fp/path]xpub key-origin form, h/H hardened markers, wpkh(...)/sh(wpkh(...)) descriptor wrappers, /<0;1>/* suffixes, #checksums. Lenient parser (never throws); all-zero fingerprint placeholder treated as unknown.
- src/lib/server/wallets.ts: createWallet() now takes fingerprint/derivationPath, ALSO parses an origin embedded in the xpub string (embedded wins), validates loudly (non-empty garbage throws a friendly error instead of silently dropping — silently dropping would re-create this bug), and persists both columns. Activity event detail gains hasKeyOrigin boolean for admin-log diagnosis.
- src/routes/api/wallets/+server.ts: POST accepts { deviceType, fingerprint, derivationPath } and descriptor-form xpub.
- Wizard (src/routes/(app)/wallets/new/): device connect (Trezor/Ledger/BitBox02/Jade) and ColdCard file import now KEEP the fingerprint+path the readers already returned (they were being discarded — the root cause); paste form accepts [fp/path]xpub in the key field plus an optional 8-hex master-fingerprint field for bare keys; QR descriptor scans pick the origin up via the same preview parse; restore-from-backup re-attaches masterFingerprint/derivationPath from the config file; Preview step shows the captured fingerprint badge; origin survives reloads via the wizardProgress sessionStorage snapshot.
- No DB migration needed: master_fingerprint/derivation_path columns already existed (db.ts lines ~108-112); they were just never written.

Verified (unit/integration tests, all green — 1468 passed / 1 pre-existing skip; svelte-check 0 errors 0 warnings):
- createWallet stores origin from explicit fields, from descriptor form, treats 00000000 as unknown, rejects malformed input, and stores null for bare xpub (regression guard).
- END-TO-END in wallets.test.ts: createWallet("[73c5da0a/84'/0'/0']zpub...") -> wallet row -> origin built exactly as transactions.ts buildDraft does -> constructPsbt -> psbtHasKeyOrigin true AND the real trezorSignRequestFromPsbt (the driver that used to throw "missing the key-origin information") accepts it, deriving address_n [84',0',0',0,0]. Inverse test pins the pre-fix state: bare-xpub wallet -> origin-free PSBT -> driver throws /key-origin/.
- psbt.test.ts: new explicit regression test that origin:null emits NO bip32Derivation on any input/output (constructPsbt itself was already correct — the bug was purely that origin was always null upstream).

Manual HW-emulator note: trezor-user-env stack is partially up on this machine (bridge 21325, ws 9001, electrs 60401, bitcoind 18443) but Trezor Connect requires a browser popup, so no headless manual signing run was performed — verification is via the unit/integration tests above, which exercise the real driver translation layer.

NOT touched: multisig paths (unaffected; MultisigSignKey already stores fp), transactions.ts, psbt.ts (verified correct, read-only). Unblocks cairn-fdlf.1 — the single-sig key-add flow now has a real key-origin capture seam: acceptReadKey(xpub, method, {fingerprint, path}) in +page.svelte, and the deviceRead.ts readers already return the full DeviceKey {xpub, fingerprint, path}.

title: Explorer search: live-suggestion dropdown visually overlaps/collides with 'Recent' search history row
description:
Page: /explorer (search box)

What I did:
- Typed a partial query ("127") into the Explorer search box while a "Recent:" search-history row was already showing below it (from earlier searches in the session).

What happened:
- The live-suggestion dropdown ("Block 127") visually overlaps the "Recent: ..." pills row directly underneath it, producing a garbled look — a small dark chip with cut-off text ("Blo...") appears to sit half on top of, half beside the "Recent" label, rather than either replacing it cleanly or pushing it down.
- Inspected via computed styles: `.live-suggest` (the suggestion box) is `position: absolute; z-index: 20; background: rgb(44,39,36)`, with a bounding rect spanning the full search-box width (248–1132px) and covering y=360.8–404.3. Meanwhile `.recent` (the search-history row) is `position: static; z-index: auto; background: transparent`, sitting almost exactly in the same region (y=364.8–390.8). Despite `.live-suggest` computing to a full-width solid-background box that should cleanly cover `.recent`, the actual rendered paint only shows a small "Blo" chip — suggesting the visible content inside `.live-suggest` doesn't fill its own bounding box, letting the transparent `.recent` row bleed through around it.
- Reproduced cleanly on a fresh, non-crashed page load (confirmed this is unrelated to the each_key_duplicate crash filed separately as cairn-5n3b).

What should have happened:
- The suggestion dropdown should either fully and cleanly cover the recent-searches row while open (solid background across its whole box) or the two should be laid out so they never occupy the same space (e.g. recent-searches hidden while suggestions are showing, or suggestions positioned below the recent row).

Priority: P3 — purely cosmetic, doesn't block search functionality (selecting the suggestion or pressing Enter still works), but looks broken/glitchy on a page every user will use constantly.
close_reason: Fixed: .recent now renders only when !liveLoading && !liveResult, making it mutually exclusive with the absolute .live-suggest dropdown — the overlap is structurally impossible. Verified by inspection + npm run check (0 errors); did not exercise in-browser because the concurrent account-recovery workflow is rewriting auth code and a login mid-flight can't be trusted.

title: Copy-to-clipboard affordance is invisible on touch devices
description: src/lib/components/CopyText.svelte: the copy icon is opacity:0 by default and only reaches opacity:1 on :hover. Touch devices have no hover state, so every copyable value in the app (addresses, tx ids, block hashes — this component is used everywhere) gives mobile users zero visual indication it's tappable. Verified live: on a 375px viewport the copy icon is confirmed opacity 0 with no other affordance. Also has no :focus-visible styling, so keyboard users tabbing to it get a focus ring around an invisible icon. Fix: give the icon a low-opacity baseline visibility (not fully hidden) instead of hover-only, and add a proper :focus-visible state matching the app's global focus style.
close_reason: Fixed in 6f1ce4f: copy icon has a 0.45 baseline opacity (full on hover/focus) so touch users see the affordance, plus a focus-visible accent ring on the button

title: BitBox02 and Jade hardware wallet support
description: Two new devices following the established signer contract. BitBox02 (Shift Crypto): WebHID, touch confirmation, Bitcoin-only firmware supported, native PSBT signing. Jade (Blockstream): BOTH modes per Alex — QR air-gapped (likely already works via the generic BBQr/UR signer; verify + add device-specific instructions) and USB via Web Serial. Bastion has working implementations of both (public/js/bitbox.js, public/js/jade.js — jade via Web Serial + jadets lib; both devices register multisig quorums in a 16-slot on-device store and return signed PSBTs directly, no server seam). Device picker cards + per-device instructions + wizard key-import + multisig registration flows + tests.
close_reason: Epic delivered: BitBox02 (full) + Jade USB (full) drivers, signers for single-sig and multisig send flows, device-picker/wizard integration, WalletDeviceType extension, WASM build support. Jade-QR (BC-UR) split into its own follow-on bead per the plan.

title: Login succeeds server-side but session cookie doesn't stick -> silent nothing-happens loop; show a clear error
description: The v0.1.1 Secure-cookie bug showed the failure mode: POST /api/auth/login/password returns 200 + Set-Cookie, the browser drops the cookie (e.g. Secure flag over plain HTTP), and the login page just loops with no feedback. Defense-in-depth fix on the login client: after a successful login response, verify the session actually stuck (an authenticated probe); if not, show a clear error like 'Login failed - your connection may not support secure cookies. Contact your administrator.' instead of silently reloading the form.
close_reason: Fixed: the login page now probes /api/auth/me after a successful password or passkey login; a definite 401 (cookie didn't stick) shows 'Login succeeded, but your browser did not keep the session cookie — the connection may not support secure cookies. Contact your administrator (on a plain-HTTP server, ORIGIN or PROTOCOL_HEADER needs to be set).' instead of silently looping. Network-blip probe failures give the login the benefit of the doubt. Verified in the browser: normal login unaffected; stubbed 401 probe renders the error.

title: Electrum TLS connections never validate server certificates (rejectUnauthorized hardcoded false)
description:
Security audit 2026-07-05 (docs/SECURITY-AUDIT-2026-07-05.md), finding F2. Verified CONFIRMED by 3/3 independent adversarial reviewers.

src/lib/server/electrum/client.ts (~lines 146-153): when connecting via TLS to the configured Electrum server, rejectUnauthorized is hardcoded to false with no configuration path (env var, settings field, or cert-pinning option) to enable real certificate validation. ElectrumClientOptions exposes only host/port/tls(boolean)/timeoutMs -- no verification-mode or pinned-fingerprint field exists anywhere. This applies identically to the default public server (electrum.blockstream.info) and to any admin-configured 'custom' server, including a self-hoster's own trusted node reached over an untrusted network path.

Impact: any network-level attacker able to intercept the path to the configured Electrum host (hostile Wi-Fi, compromised router/DNS, malicious VPN/Tor exit, compromised ISP) can perform a full TLS MITM using a self-signed certificate, and Cairn accepts it silently. This is an integrity/availability risk, not primarily a confidentiality one (the in-code comment 'no secrets, only public chain data' is true for eavesdropping but ignores active tampering): a MITM on this channel can feed Cairn forged balances/UTXO sets/history/fee estimates, or intercept and lie about blockchain.transaction.broadcast results (see companion finding on unverified broadcast txids). No private keys or PSBT signing material transit this channel, bounding the severity below direct fund theft.

Fix: default to rejectUnauthorized: true, with an explicit opt-out (documented, off by default) for admins who knowingly run a self-signed server; consider certificate/public-key pinning (TOFU: pin fingerprint on first connect, alert on change) as a stronger alternative that still supports self-signed setups safely.
close_reason: Fixed in security-audit remediation pass; see commit. All 897 tests pass, typecheck clean.

title: Portfolio balance chart can show duplicate x-axis date labels
description:
Dashboard "Balance over time" chart (src/lib/components/portfolio/BalanceChart.svelte)
picks up to 5 x-axis ticks by evenly-spaced INDEX into the filtered points array
(xTicks derived, ~line 132-144), then formats each with formatDate() which only
shows month+day (no time, no year) — src/lib/components/portfolio/BalanceChart.svelte:118-121.

When a wallet's balance-history series is sparse (few data points), two distinct
ticks can land on data points from the same calendar day, producing two
adjacent x-axis labels reading the exact same text (e.g. "Jul 4" ... "Jul 4" ...
"Jul 6"), which looks like a rendering bug even though each label is technically
correct for the point it's attached to.

Reproduced on the live QA instance (2026-07-06) after importing a fresh
single-key wallet with a small backfilled history — both the "1M" and "ALL"
ranges showed this ("ALL" screenshot: ticks read "Jul 4", "Jul 4", "Jul 6").

Not a data-correctness bug — a display/UX issue: identical adjacent tick labels
give the reader no signal that these are two different points in time.

Fix sketch: when two chosen ticks fall on the same calendar day, either skip the
redundant one, or fall back to a date+time format for that pair, or space ticks
by roughly-even time delta rather than by raw array index so sparse/clustered
data doesn't produce duplicate-looking labels.
close_reason: Fixed in src/lib/components/portfolio/BalanceChart.svelte xTicks: ticks are now spaced by roughly-even TIME across the visible range and snapped to the nearest data point (index spacing bunched ticks on sparse series), then deduped — a tick landing on the same data point or the same calendar-day label as the previous kept tick is skipped rather than reformatted, so every rendered label is distinct. svelte-check clean, full suite green.

title: Orphaned sign-session roster code (getRoster, RosterMember, RosterStatus) -- wire in or delete
description: src/lib/server/multisigRoster.ts:111,198-234's getRoster() function and RosterMember/RosterStatus types are documented as "what the sign-session view renders" but no route or page calls them anywhere -- the only caller is the function's own test. getSignableMultisig() in wallets/multisig.ts:167 implements a different, simpler per-transaction gate against the same table instead. Looks like a sign-session roster UI that was built but never wired to a route (possibly related to the collaborative-custody work). Fix: either wire getRoster() into the multisig sign-session page, or delete it and its test. Found in the 2026-07-05 tech-debt audit (docs/TECH-DEBT-AUDIT-2026-07-05.md).
close_reason: Wired getRoster() into the multisig send page (Unit resolution: 'wire in', not delete). The send loader reconciles the frozen roster against the live PSBT and, for SHARED wallets only (>1 roster member), passes a per-person roster to the sign step, which now renders a 'who signed / who is waiting' list beside the existing per-key chips. Solo wallets unchanged. getRoster/RosterMember are no longer orphaned — they power exactly the collaborative-custody sign-session view they were built for. Typecheck clean.

title: Rename-recovery migration drops child tables without verifying they're empty too
description:
src/lib/server/db.ts (~lines 193-207): the empty-shell rename-recovery migration (45c8381, a genuine and well-designed fix for a real near-miss data-loss incident -- recovered a live install's 6 multisig wallets, 21 keys, 11 transactions) only checks rowCount('multisigs') === 0 before dropping multisig_keys, multisig_transactions, ledger_multisig_registrations, AND multisigs. The comment claims it 'only ever discards provably-empty tables', but only the parent table's emptiness is independently verified -- the children are assumed empty because the parent is.

In the current schema this is very likely always safe in practice: PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON is set at line 14, well before this migration runs, and the child tables presumably FK-reference multisigs(id), which under normal SQLite operation makes it schema-impossible for a child to have rows while its parent has none. But this is a DROP TABLE -- irreversible -- in a migration whose entire purpose is preventing exactly this kind of data loss, so relying transitively on FK enforcement rather than an explicit, independent emptiness check on each child table is worth tightening. Add the same rowCount(t) === 0 guard to each child table before dropping it, matching the parent's already-explicit check, so the safety claim in the comment is actually true rather than assumed. Low priority -- defense in depth on an already-safe-in-practice path, not a live bug.
close_reason: Fixed in 5a09851: the empty-shell recovery now independently verifies every shell table (parent + all children) is empty before the irreversible DROP, aborting with a warning if any child holds rows while the parent is empty. Comment updated to describe the now-verified behavior.

title: No pino redact config -- add defense-in-depth for secrets/xpubs/PSBTs/mnemonics
description: src/lib/server/logger.ts:184-192's pino() call has no redact option configured at all. The only redaction anywhere (redactPath() in hooks.server.ts) covers only txids/addresses in URL path segments, not log-call metadata objects. No current call site is known to log a secret, but nothing would stop a future logger.error call from writing a live SMTP password, xpub, PSBT, or recovery phrase into data/logs/cairn.log, which /admin/logs then serves over HTTP. Fix: add a redact config covering password/pass/passEnc/token/secret/accessToken/xprv/mnemonic/phrase/psbt style fields as defense-in-depth. Found in the 2026-07-05 tech-debt audit (docs/TECH-DEBT-AUDIT-2026-07-05.md).
close_reason: Fixed in 7ebd68c

title: RecipientCombobox renders duplicate DOM ids when multiple instances mount
description: Batch sending mounts one RecipientCombobox per recipient row; each hardcodes id=recipient and id=recipient-listbox internally, producing duplicate DOM ids (invalid HTML, aria-activedescendant ambiguity). Parameterize the ids via a prop (e.g. id={base}-N). Found by the batch-send build agent; functionality currently works.
close_reason: Fixed: RecipientCombobox takes an id prop (default 'recipient') deriving listbox/option ids; send page passes recipient-{row.key} per row and the single-row label targets it. check 0 errors.

title: notified_txids rows orphaned forever: no FK to users/wallets/multisigs, never cleaned on delete or factory reset
description: src/lib/server/db.ts ~L606-616: CREATE TABLE notified_txids has user_id INTEGER NOT NULL and wallet_id INTEGER NOT NULL but NEITHER column has a REFERENCES/FK clause (every other user_id column in db.ts uses REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE). Grepped the whole codebase (addressWatcher.ts is the only writer) and there is no DELETE FROM notified_txids anywhere — not in deleteUser(), not in wallet/multisig deletion paths, not in admin.ts resetInstance(). Effect: every row this table has ever written for a deleted user, wallet, or multisig becomes a permanent orphan. resetInstance() (src/lib/server/admin.ts) explicitly documents itself as a full factory reset ('delete every user, session, wallet, invite and setting') and the Settings UI (admin/settings/+page.svelte) tells the admin 'nothing else survives' — but notified_txids rows survive indefinitely with no cleanup path at all, even outside of factory reset (normal single-user/wallet deletion leaks rows too). Fix: add ON DELETE CASCADE-capable cleanup — either an FK (tricky since wallet_id is polymorphic across wallet_kind) or explicit DELETE FROM notified_txids WHERE user_id = ? calls alongside deleteUser()/wallet deletion/resetInstance().
close_reason: Fixed in 46dd16f

title: Single-sig wallets never persist master_fingerprint/derivation_path — HW signing broken for all single-key wallets
description:
createWallet() (src/lib/server/wallets.ts:138-190) inserts only (user_id, name, type, xpub, script_type, device_type) — master_fingerprint and derivation_path (both declared in WalletRow, real DB columns) are never written, by ANY creation path, including the real hardware-connect flow: readSingleSigKeyFromTrezor() (src/lib/hw/trezor.ts:1346+) correctly reads {xpub, fingerprint, path} from the device, but src/routes/(app)/wallets/new/+page.svelte only captures d.xpub into validatedXpub — fingerprint/path are discarded, and the submitted form only sends xpub + deviceType. setWalletDevice() (wallets.ts:197) likewise only ever touches device_type.

Consequence: constructPsbt() (src/lib/server/bitcoin/psbt.ts:509) only emits bip32Derivation when params.origin is given, and src/lib/server/transactions.ts:299 only builds origin when wallet.master_fingerprint is non-null — which is NEVER true for a single-key wallet. Every exported/signed PSBT for a single-sig wallet therefore has no bip32Derivation. Cairn's own signPsbtWithTrezor() explicitly requires it and throws 'This transaction is missing the key-origin information the Trezor needs to sign. Re-create it from a wallet set up with its master fingerprint.' (trezor.ts ~line 176). The same requirement almost certainly applies to the Ledger/BitBox02/Jade signers (same PSBT, same guard pattern).

Reproduced live on the v0.1.3 Umbrel instance (http://umbrel.local:3211): confirmed via source read plus a live download+decode of a real 'QA Trezor (regtest)' wallet's unsigned PSBT (src/routes/api/wallets/[id]/transactions/[txId]/file/+server.ts output) — the input has witnessUtxo/nonWitnessUtxo but no bip32Derivation field at all.

Note: multisig keys (multisig_keys table / MultisigSignKey) DO store and validate fingerprint correctly (resolveMultisigSignKeys in trezor.ts requires it) — this bug is specific to the single-key  table/createWallet() path.

This may be a very recent regression: today's (2026-07-06) wallet-dedup refactor (see project memory 'cairn-wallet-dedup-refactor') left 'Ledger/BitBox/Jade signer pairs still unmerged' per its own notes — worth checking whether single-sig fingerprint capture regressed in that refactor or predates it.

Fix shape: thread {fingerprint, path} from the device-read result through the wallet-creation form (hidden inputs) and into createWallet()'s INSERT (and the paste-xpub path should leave both null, as today, since there's no device to trust).
close_reason: Duplicate of cairn-alw8 -- identical root cause (single-key wallets never persist master_fingerprint/derivation_path, breaking all HW signing), filed independently in another concurrent QA session against the same live instance (same pattern as cairn-tu0i, already closed as a dup of alw8 for this exact issue). See cairn-alw8 for the canonical writeup.
